subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Family Property: The Family Home Protection Act 1976, Spousal Consent, and Property Orders

questions.question: What is the legal effect under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 of a conveyance of the family home without the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse?

options.A: The conveyance is valid but voidable at the election of the non-owning spouse
options.B: The conveyance is void and has no legal effect whatsoever
options.C: The conveyance binds third parties but not the spouses
options.D: The conveyance is enforceable until set aside by the court

questions.explanation: Section 3 FHPA 1976 provides that such a conveyance is void — not merely voidable. The transaction has no legal effect whatsoever, as if it never happened. The void/voidable distinction is one of the most tested points in this area.

questions.question: What is the key word in section 2 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 when defining whether a dwelling qualifies as a family home?

options.A: Ordinarily
options.B: Primarily
options.C: Exclusively
options.D: Habitually

questions.explanation: Section 2 defines a family home as a dwelling in which a married couple ordinarily resides. The word ordinarily means primary, habitual residence. A holiday cottage or investment property will not qualify.

questions.question: In Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995], the Supreme Court held that Mrs Smyth's consent to a mortgage over the family home was invalid. What was the primary reason?

options.A: She had not signed the consent form personally
options.B: The consent was not given before the mortgage was executed
options.C: She had not been independently advised of the nature and consequences of what she was signing
options.D: The bank had failed to register the mortgage against the title

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court held that consent must be free and informed. Mrs Smyth had not received independent advice about the nature and consequences of the transaction. The bank was fixed with knowledge of the circumstances and the mortgage was held void.

questions.question: Under the Family Home Protection Act 1976, when must the non-owning spouse's consent to a conveyance of the family home be given?

options.A: Within one month of the conveyance
options.B: At any time before or after the conveyance provided it is in writing
options.C: Contemporaneously with the conveyance
options.D: Prior to the conveyance

questions.explanation: Section 3 requires prior written consent. The consent must be given before the conveyance takes place. Retrospective consent will not validate an otherwise void conveyance.

questions.question: What principle was established in Somers v Weir [1979] regarding consent under the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: Consent must be genuine, specific, and relate to the actual transaction being entered into
options.B: Consent can be given by any family member with authority to act on behalf of the non-owning spouse
options.C: A general consent given in advance is sufficient provided it covers the type of transaction concerned
options.D: Consent is presumed valid once given in writing, regardless of the circumstances

questions.explanation: Somers v Weir [1979] confirmed that consent must be genuine, specific, and relate to the actual transaction. A blanket general consent given in advance of any particular transaction will not suffice under section 3.

questions.question: Section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 permits a court to dispense with spousal consent. Which of the following is NOT a statutory ground for dispensation?

options.A: Consent is being unreasonably withheld
options.B: The non-owning spouse refuses to negotiate with the titleholder
options.C: The non-owning spouse cannot be found after reasonable enquiries
options.D: The non-owning spouse is incapable of giving consent

questions.explanation: Section 4 provides three grounds: unreasonable withholding; inability to locate the spouse after reasonable enquiries; and incapacity. A mere refusal to negotiate is not a separate statutory ground — the question is whether the withholding is unreasonable.

questions.question: In Containercare v Wycherley [1982], why did the court hold that the registration of a judgment mortgage against the family home did not require the consent of the non-owning spouse?

options.A: Judgment mortgages are not registrable against family homes under Irish law
options.B: The non-owning spouse had impliedly consented by failing to object
options.C: A judgment mortgage does not constitute a conveyance within the meaning of the Family Home Protection Act 1976
options.D: The 1976 Act applies only to voluntary transactions between the spouses

questions.explanation: The court in Containercare v Wycherley held that registering a judgment mortgage is not a conveyance within the meaning of the FHPA 1976. This created the so-called FHPA lacuna — a judgment creditor could obtain security over the family home without spousal consent.

questions.question: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 extended protections equivalent to the Family Home Protection Act 1976 to which category of persons?

options.A: Cohabiting couples of any gender equally
options.B: Civil partners only, not cohabitants
options.C: All persons in long-term residential relationships
options.D: Civil partners, with equivalent protections for the shared home

questions.explanation: The 2010 Act extended equivalent protections to civil partners in respect of the shared home, mirroring the core requirements of the 1976 Act. Cohabitants received separate, different rights under the same Act.

questions.question: Which legislation introduced property adjustment orders allowing a court to transfer property — including the family home — between spouses as part of judicial separation proceedings?

options.A: The Family Law Act 1995
options.B: The Family Home Protection Act 1976
options.C: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010
options.D: The Succession Act 1965

questions.explanation: The Family Law Act 1995 introduced property adjustment orders for judicial separation. These orders allow courts to transfer property including the family home between spouses and operate independently of the 1976 Act.

questions.question: Under which legislation can a court make property adjustment orders in the context of divorce proceedings?

options.A: Family Home Protection Act 1976
options.B: Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996
options.C: Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010
options.D: Succession Act 1965

questions.explanation: Property adjustment orders in divorce proceedings are made under the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996. These orders give courts broad discretion to achieve fairness on relationship breakdown and operate independently of the 1976 Act.

questions.question: Under the Family Home Protection Act 1976, a conveyance without the required spousal consent is correctly described as:

options.A: Voidable at the election of the non-owning spouse
options.B: Enforceable subject to court ratification
options.C: Void ab initio with no legal effect
options.D: Valid but carrying a financial penalty for the titleholder

questions.explanation: A conveyance without the required prior written consent is void — not voidable. It has no legal effect whatsoever, from the outset. This distinction is one of the most examined points in the 1976 Act.

questions.question: A titleholder spouse executes a mortgage over the family home without the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse. The bank argues that retrospective consent should validate the transaction. What is the correct legal position?

options.A: Retrospective consent is sufficient to validate the mortgage
options.B: The mortgage is void but the bank may sue the titleholder spouse
options.C: The mortgage is voidable and the bank may seek court ratification
options.D: The mortgage is void and retrospective consent cannot cure the invalidity

questions.explanation: Section 3 requires prior written consent. A mortgage executed without such consent is void from the outset. Retrospective consent cannot cure the invalidity — the section expressly requires consent to precede the conveyance.

questions.question: A married couple owns a holiday cottage and a family home in which they both ordinarily reside. Which property is protected by the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: Only the family home in which they ordinarily reside
options.B: Both properties equally
options.C: Neither property, as both are jointly owned
options.D: The holiday cottage if it has greater market value

questions.explanation: Under section 2, the 1976 Act protects only the dwelling in which the married couple ordinarily resides — their primary, habitual residence. A holiday cottage does not satisfy the ordinary residence requirement and is not protected.

questions.question: What was the outcome in Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995] in relation to the mortgage over the family home?

options.A: The court upheld the mortgage as Mrs Smyth had signed the consent form
options.B: The Supreme Court held the mortgage was void as Mrs Smyth's consent was not free and informed
options.C: The court held the mortgage was voidable and set it aside in its discretion
options.D: The court held the bank had taken all reasonable steps to obtain valid consent

questions.explanation: The Supreme Court held the mortgage was void. Mrs Smyth had not been independently advised of the nature and consequences of what she was signing. The bank was fixed with knowledge of the circumstances, and her consent was not free and informed.

questions.question: For consent under the Family Home Protection Act 1976 to be valid following Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995], it must be:

options.A: Witnessed by a solicitor acting for the titleholder
options.B: Registered in the Land Registry with the mortgage deed
options.C: Free and informed, with the non-owning spouse independently advised of the nature and consequences
options.D: Given personally to the purchaser or lender rather than through an intermediary

questions.explanation: Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995] SC established that consent must be free and informed. The non-owning spouse must understand the nature and consequences of the transaction. Independent legal advice from a solicitor acting for the spouse alone is the standard mechanism.

questions.question: In Somers v Weir [1979], the court rejected a general advance consent given by the non-owning spouse. What principle does this establish?

options.A: Consent must be registered to be effective
options.B: Consent must always be given through the Land Registry
options.C: Consent can be validly given in advance provided it is in writing
options.D: A blanket general consent given in advance of any particular transaction is insufficient under the 1976 Act

questions.explanation: Somers v Weir established that consent must be genuine, specific, and relate to the actual transaction. A blanket or pre-emptive consent — however clearly worded — does not satisfy section 3. The non-owning spouse must know precisely what they are consenting to.

questions.question: Section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 provides a safety valve allowing the court to dispense with spousal consent. Which of the following is a valid ground for such an order?

options.A: The non-owning spouse is unreasonably withholding consent
options.B: The titleholder spouse requires the proceeds of sale to discharge business debts
options.C: The non-owning spouse has agreed orally but refuses to sign written consent
options.D: The sale is required to settle the estate of a deceased relative

questions.explanation: Section 4 permits the court to dispense with consent where it is being unreasonably withheld. Financial necessity of the titleholder is not, by itself, a ground. The three grounds are: unreasonable withholding; inability to locate the spouse; and incapacity.

questions.question: Under section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, the court may dispense with spousal consent where the non-owning spouse:

options.A: Has not responded to the consent request within a reasonable time
options.B: Cannot be found after reasonable enquiries
options.C: Is residing abroad at the time the consent is required
options.D: Has ceased to use the family home as their primary residence

questions.explanation: One of the three statutory grounds under section 4 is that the non-owning spouse cannot be found after reasonable enquiries. Mere absence abroad or failure to respond within a reasonable time would not automatically satisfy this ground without genuine efforts to locate the spouse.

questions.question: Which of the following is also a statutory ground under section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 allowing the court to dispense with spousal consent?

options.A: The non-owning spouse has permanently emigrated
options.B: The titleholder can demonstrate the transaction is commercially necessary
options.C: The non-owning spouse is incapable of giving consent
options.D: The non-owning spouse has been absent from the family home for more than twelve months

questions.explanation: Section 4 provides three grounds: unreasonable withholding; inability to locate the spouse; and incapacity. Incapacity — such as through serious illness or mental incapacity — is a recognised statutory ground. Absence or emigration alone does not suffice.

questions.question: The decision in Containercare v Wycherley [1982] identified a significant gap in the protection offered by the Family Home Protection Act 1976. What is this gap commonly called?

options.A: The mortgagee lacuna
options.B: The title lacuna
options.C: The conveyance lacuna
options.D: The FHPA lacuna

questions.explanation: The gap identified in Containercare v Wycherley — that the registration of a judgment mortgage does not constitute a conveyance within the Act — is commonly called the FHPA lacuna. A judgment creditor can obtain security over the family home without needing spousal consent.

questions.question: What is the practical consequence of the FHPA lacuna identified in Containercare v Wycherley [1982]?

options.A: A judgment creditor can register a judgment mortgage against the family home without the non-owning spouse's consent
options.B: A judgment creditor cannot enforce any security against the family home
options.C: A judgment mortgage against the family home is automatically void
options.D: The non-owning spouse can apply to court to have the judgment mortgage set aside as of right

questions.explanation: Because a judgment mortgage is not a conveyance within the FHPA 1976, a judgment creditor can register it against the family home without the non-owning spouse's consent. This is a notable limitation of the Act's protective reach.

questions.question: Under section 2 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, which scenario brings a property within the definition of a family home?

options.A: A property in which the couple resided previously but no longer occupy
options.B: A property in which one spouse ordinarily resides and the other has left but has not been ordered by a court to leave
options.C: A property held as a rental investment in which the family resided for a period
options.D: A property which the spouses intend to make their family home in the future

questions.explanation: Section 2 includes within the definition a dwelling where one spouse ordinarily resides and the other has left but has not been ordered by a court to leave. This protects the remaining spouse where the couple has separated informally.

questions.question: Which of the following transactions relating to the family home requires the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: The titleholder spouse taking out a bank loan secured on other property
options.B: The titleholder spouse leasing a parking space adjacent to the family home
options.C: The titleholder spouse granting a mortgage over the family home to a bank
options.D: The titleholder spouse making the non-owning spouse a co-owner of the family home

questions.explanation: A mortgage over the family home constitutes a conveyance of an interest in the home and therefore requires the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse under section 3. Transactions that do not convey an interest in the home itself are not caught by the Act.

questions.question: The Civil Partnership and Certain Rights and Obligations of Cohabitants Act 2010 extended protections to civil partners. What term does the 2010 Act use for the civil partners' home?

options.A: Principal private residence
options.B: Domestic dwelling
options.C: Family residence
options.D: Shared home

questions.explanation: The 2010 Act uses the term shared home when referring to the civil partners' residence. The statutory framework mirrors the 1976 Act in its core requirements — the prior written consent of the non-owning civil partner is required before any conveyance of the shared home.

questions.question: A titleholder spouse sells the family home to a developer without obtaining the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse. What is the effect of the sale on the developer's title?

options.A: The sale is void and the developer acquires no title
options.B: The sale is voidable and the developer holds provisional title until court proceedings are issued
options.C: The sale is valid and the developer takes the property free of the non-owning spouse's claim
options.D: The sale is binding between the parties but void against the non-owning spouse only

questions.explanation: A conveyance without the required prior written consent under section 3 is void — it has no legal effect whatsoever. The developer acquires no title, regardless of whether they were aware of the absent consent. This underscores the importance of checking spousal consent in every conveyancing transaction.

questions.question: Under section 2 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, where one spouse has left the family home, the property continues to be a family home protected by the Act unless:

options.A: More than twelve months have elapsed since the departure
options.B: The departing spouse has been ordered by a court to leave
options.C: The departing spouse has established a new ordinary residence elsewhere
options.D: The remaining spouse has applied for and obtained sole ownership

questions.explanation: Section 2 provides that where one spouse has left, the dwelling remains a family home unless the departing spouse has been ordered by a court to leave. Voluntary departure does not remove the property from the Act's protection.

questions.question: Following Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995], what is the most reliable way for a lending institution to ensure that spousal consent to a mortgage over the family home is valid?

options.A: Obtaining the consent in the presence of a bank official
options.B: Having the mortgage deed witnessed by a commissioner for oaths
options.C: Ensuring the non-owning spouse receives independent legal advice about the nature and consequences of the transaction
options.D: Registering the consent form in the Land Registry prior to execution

questions.explanation: Bank of Ireland v Smyth established that consent must be free and informed. In practice, lending institutions satisfy this by ensuring the non-owning spouse receives independent legal advice from a solicitor acting for the spouse alone — not the lender or the titleholder — before signing.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the operation of property adjustment orders under the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996?

options.A: They allow the court to impose a co-ownership arrangement on the family home
options.B: They require the consent of both spouses before the court can act
options.C: They give the court discretion to direct financial compensation only, not property transfer
options.D: They give the court broad discretion to transfer property including the family home between spouses on divorce

questions.explanation: Property adjustment orders under the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996 give courts broad discretion to transfer property — including the family home — between spouses as part of divorce proceedings. These orders operate independently of the 1976 Act and aim to achieve fairness on relationship breakdown.

questions.question: A titleholder spouse purports to sell the family home to a purchaser. Under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, at what point must the non-owning spouse's consent be obtained?

options.A: Prior to the conveyance — the consent must precede it
options.B: Within twenty-one days of the conveyance being registered
options.C: At any time before the sale closes, including on completion day
options.D: Upon the non-owning spouse issuing a formal notice of objection

questions.explanation: Section 3 requires prior written consent — the consent must be given before the conveyance. The protection is prospective. Once the conveyance is executed without consent it is void, and there is no mechanism for retrospective ratification.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the distinction between void and voidable in the context of the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: A void conveyance can be validated by court order; a voidable conveyance cannot
options.B: A void conveyance has no legal effect from the outset; a voidable conveyance is valid until set aside
options.C: A void conveyance is effective against bona fide purchasers; a voidable conveyance is not
options.D: A void conveyance requires court proceedings to be set aside; a voidable conveyance is automatically ineffective

questions.explanation: Section 3 makes a non-consensual conveyance void — not voidable. A void conveyance has no legal effect from the outset, as if it never happened. A voidable conveyance is valid and effective unless and until set aside by a court. The distinction is fundamental and heavily tested.

questions.question: The Supreme Court decision in Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995] had which of the following consequences for the bank?

options.A: The bank was awarded a right to sue the titleholder spouse for its losses
options.B: The court directed the non-owning spouse to pay compensation to the bank
options.C: The mortgage was held void and the bank was fixed with knowledge of the circumstances
options.D: The bank retained priority over any subsequent registered interest

questions.explanation: In Bank of Ireland v Smyth, the Supreme Court held the mortgage void because the consent was not free and informed. The bank was fixed with knowledge of the circumstances — it could not take advantage of the consent form where it knew or had constructive notice that it was not validly obtained.

questions.question: The Containercare v Wycherley [1982] decision established that the FHPA 1976 does not extend to judgment mortgages. What does this reveal about the interpretation of the word conveyance in the Act?

options.A: A conveyance requires voluntary transfer agreed by both spouses
options.B: A conveyance must be registered in the Land Registry to take effect
options.C: A conveyance includes only dispositions made by the titleholder voluntarily
options.D: The term conveyance in the Act is interpreted narrowly to exclude registration of a judgment mortgage

questions.explanation: The court in Containercare v Wycherley held that conveyance in section 3 does not extend to the registration of a judgment mortgage. This narrow interpretation creates the FHPA lacuna — judgment creditors can obtain security over the family home without the consent of the non-owning spouse.

questions.question: What is the most significant practical consequence of the FHPA lacuna identified in Containercare v Wycherley [1982]?

options.A: A judgment creditor can effectively bypass the consent protections of the 1976 Act by registering a judgment mortgage against the family home
options.B: A judgment creditor cannot enforce any judgment against property held as a family home
options.C: The Family Home Protection Act 1976 has no application to commercial debts
options.D: Judgment mortgages are treated as void against the non-owning spouse

questions.explanation: The FHPA lacuna means the protective purpose of the Act — preventing the non-owning spouse from being dispossessed without consent — can be circumvented by a judgment creditor registering a judgment mortgage against the family home without any consent from the non-owning spouse.

questions.question: Under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, which formal requirements must the spousal consent satisfy?

options.A: It must be witnessed by a commissioner for oaths
options.B: It must be in writing and given prior to the conveyance
options.C: It must be registered in the Property Registration Authority
options.D: It must be renewed if more than six months elapses before the conveyance takes place

questions.explanation: Section 3 requires that the consent be in writing and given prior to the conveyance. Oral consent is insufficient. The written consent must precede the transaction — retrospective or contemporaneous consent will not satisfy the section.

questions.question: The finding in Somers v Weir [1979] that consent must be genuine, specific, and relate to the actual transaction means which of the following?

options.A: Consent must always be given in the presence of both parties to the transaction
options.B: Consent must be witnessed by a solicitor acting for the non-owning spouse
options.C: The non-owning spouse must be aware of the precise details of the transaction to which they are consenting
options.D: Consent given to a class of transactions is valid provided the specific transaction falls within that class

questions.explanation: The principle from Somers v Weir is that consent must be tailored to the specific transaction. The non-owning spouse must know precisely what they are consenting to — a blanket or pre-emptive consent that does not identify the specific transaction fails this test.

questions.question: Under section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, the obligation to obtain prior written spousal consent applies in which of the following scenarios?

options.A: Only where the family home was purchased during the marriage
options.B: Only where both spouses are named on the title to the family home
options.C: Only where the sale price is below market value
options.D: Wherever one spouse holds legal title to the family home and proposes to convey an interest in it

questions.explanation: Section 3 applies whenever the titleholder spouse — whoever holds the legal title — proposes to convey an interest in the family home. The Act is designed to protect the non-owning spouse regardless of when the property was acquired or what the sale price is.

questions.question: Property adjustment orders under the Family Law Act 1995 are made in the context of which type of proceedings?

options.A: Judicial separation proceedings
options.B: Divorce proceedings
options.C: Nullity proceedings
options.D: Both judicial separation and divorce proceedings

questions.explanation: The Family Law Act 1995 introduced property adjustment orders in the context of judicial separation. Property adjustment orders in divorce proceedings are made under the Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996. Each Act governs a different form of relationship breakdown.

questions.question: The Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996 introduced which type of court order in relation to property on divorce?

options.A: A family home protection order
options.B: A property adjustment order
options.C: A beneficial interest declaration
options.D: A shared home injunction

questions.explanation: The Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996 introduced property adjustment orders in the context of divorce. These orders allow the court to transfer property — including the family home — between spouses and operate independently of the Family Home Protection Act 1976.

questions.question: Section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 is described as a safety valve. What does this mean in practice?

options.A: It allows the court to impose a lower sale price to protect the non-owning spouse
options.B: It restricts the non-owning spouse from applying more than once to prevent a sale
options.C: It prevents the 1976 Act from giving the non-owning spouse an absolute veto by allowing court dispensation of consent
options.D: It limits the court's power to set aside void conveyances after a limitation period has expired

questions.explanation: Section 4 is a safety valve because it prevents the 1976 Act from operating as an absolute veto. Where consent is unreasonably withheld, the court can substitute its order. The Act is designed to give the non-owning spouse a genuine right to be heard — not a weapon to frustrate legitimate transactions.

questions.question: Which of the following requirements concerning consent under the Family Home Protection Act 1976 is correct?

options.A: Oral consent is sufficient provided two witnesses attest to it
options.B: Consent given by a solicitor on behalf of the non-owning spouse is always valid
options.C: Written consent given to a general class of transactions covers all transactions within that class
options.D: Consent must be in writing and must be given before the conveyance takes place

questions.explanation: Section 3 is clear: consent must be in writing and must be given prior to the conveyance. Oral consent, blanket consents, and retrospective consent are all insufficient. This is a strict formal requirement with no exceptions.

questions.question: In which year was Bank of Ireland v Smyth decided by the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Bank of Ireland v Smyth was decided by the Supreme Court in 1995. It remains the leading Irish authority on the requirement that consent under the Family Home Protection Act 1976 must be free and informed, establishing the standard of independent legal advice for the non-owning spouse.

questions.question: In which year was Containercare v Wycherley decided?
questions.explanation: Containercare v Wycherley was decided in 1982. It established that the registration of a judgment mortgage against the family home does not constitute a conveyance within the meaning of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, creating what is known as the FHPA lacuna.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Family Home Protection Act 1976 is INCORRECT?

options.A: Consent must be given in writing
options.B: Consent must be given prior to the conveyance
options.C: The sole owner spouse may sell the family home without consent if the sale is at market value
options.D: A conveyance without the required consent is void

questions.explanation: The Family Home Protection Act 1976 applies regardless of the consideration or price achieved. The sole owner spouse cannot sell the family home without the prior written consent of the non-owning spouse, irrespective of whether the transaction is at market value. Statement C is incorrect as a matter of law.

questions.question: A titleholder spouse obtains the agreement of the non-owning spouse by telephone, who confirms orally that they agree to the proposed mortgage. Is this consent valid under the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: Yes, provided the telephone call is recorded
options.B: Yes, if the non-owning spouse later confirms in writing
options.C: Yes, as the non-owning spouse has clearly expressed their agreement
options.D: No, because section 3 requires consent to be in writing — oral consent is insufficient

questions.explanation: Section 3 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 expressly requires the consent to be in writing. An oral agreement — however clearly expressed — does not satisfy the statutory requirement. The non-owning spouse must provide written consent before the conveyance takes place.

questions.question: What is the effect of a void conveyance under the Family Home Protection Act 1976 on a bona fide purchaser for value without notice of the absent consent?

options.A: A void conveyance has no legal effect even against a bona fide purchaser for value without notice
options.B: A bona fide purchaser for value without notice is protected and acquires good title
options.C: The purchaser acquires an equitable interest only
options.D: The purchaser must seek an indemnity from the titleholder spouse

questions.explanation: Because the conveyance is void — not voidable — it has no legal effect whatsoever, including against a bona fide purchaser for value without notice. This is a critical consequence of the void/voidable distinction: the usual equitable protections for innocent purchasers do not apply to void transactions.

questions.question: What was the legislative purpose of the Family Home Protection Act 1976?

options.A: To create automatic joint ownership of all family assets on marriage
options.B: To prevent the non-owning spouse from being dispossessed of the family home without their prior written consent
options.C: To give the courts power to distribute all family assets on relationship breakdown
options.D: To abolish the right of a titleholder spouse to deal with real property during a marriage

questions.explanation: The 1976 Act was enacted to address the injustice whereby a non-owning spouse had no automatic right to prevent the titleholder from selling or mortgaging the family home. Its purpose is to require the non-owning spouse's prior written consent before any conveyance of an interest in the family home.

questions.question: Following Bank of Ireland v Smyth [1995], in what circumstances is independent legal advice for the non-owning spouse particularly important?

options.A: Only where the non-owning spouse has a beneficial interest under a trust
options.B: Only where the property is mortgaged for commercial rather than residential purposes
options.C: Wherever consent to a conveyance of the family home is sought, to ensure the consent is free and informed
options.D: Only where the mortgage exceeds a specified threshold in value

questions.explanation: Bank of Ireland v Smyth established that the non-owning spouse must give free and informed consent. Independent legal advice from a solicitor acting for the spouse alone is the standard mechanism for satisfying this requirement. It applies whenever consent to a conveyance of the family home is sought.

questions.question: Property adjustment orders under the Family Law Act 1995 and Family Law (Divorce) Act 1996 give the court:

options.A: Power to impose a shared tenancy arrangement on the spouses
options.B: Power to make recommendations subject to the parties' agreement
options.C: Power to vary the terms of any existing mortgage over the family home
options.D: Broad discretion to transfer property between spouses including the family home to achieve fairness

questions.explanation: Property adjustment orders under both the 1995 and 1996 Acts give courts broad discretion to transfer property — including the family home — between spouses. The orders operate independently of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 and are aimed at achieving fairness on relationship breakdown.

questions.question: Under section 2 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976, a property qualifies as a family home where:

options.A: Either the couple ordinarily resides there, or one spouse resides there and the other has left but has not been ordered by a court to leave
options.B: The property is registered in the names of both spouses at the Land Registry
options.C: The spouses have lived in the property at any point during the marriage
options.D: The property is occupied by the couple for at least six months per year

questions.explanation: Section 2 defines a family home as a dwelling where the married couple ordinarily resides, or where one spouse ordinarily resides and the other has left but has not been ordered by a court to leave. The ordinary residence requirement means it must be their primary, habitual residence.

questions.question: In assessing under section 4 of the Family Home Protection Act 1976 whether consent is being unreasonably withheld, what does the court consider?

options.A: The commercial viability of the proposed transaction alone
options.B: All the circumstances, including the interests of the non-owning spouse and any dependent children of the family
options.C: Only the financial interests of the titleholder spouse
options.D: Whether the non-owning spouse has received independent legal advice

questions.explanation: In assessing a section 4 application, the court takes into account all the circumstances — including the reasons for withholding consent, the interests of the non-owning spouse, and the welfare of any dependent children. The section balances the non-owning spouse's genuine right to be heard against the need to avoid an unreasonable veto.
